content.full_text:
State of California Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

December 21, 1981

Melinda Luedtke
Director
Department of Economic
and Business Development
1030 13th Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Melinda:

In response to your questions: If an out-of-state trip
for the director of your office of international trade is paid
for by a group of businesses, he or she would have to report
such a trip as a gift or income received from such businesses
on his or her annual Statement of Economic Interests. If a
foreign government pays for such a trip that too would have to be
disclosed as a gift or income. See Sections 87207, 87300, and
87313 of the Government Code, copies attached.

In addition, if the trip is worth $250 or more, then the
director of the international trade office would be required to
disqualify himself or herself from participating in any decision
which would have a material financial effect -- distinguishable
from the public generally -- on the businesses or foreign government involved. For example, he or she could not participate
in negotiations which would lead to higher quotas, less shipping
expenses, higher prices, etc. for the commodity produced by the
companies involved in the gift. See Government Code Sections 87100
and 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, copies attached.

I hope this information is helpful. You may also wish to
consult the guidelines for out-of-state travel promulgated by
the Department of Finance.

Sincerely,

Tom K. Houston
Chairman

TKH:nrw

sections.question_synthetic: Must a state director disclose and separately report out-of-state trips paid for by business groups or foreign governments, and what are the conflict of interest implications of accepting such trips?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, such trips must be reported as gifts or income on the Statement of Economic Interests. If the trip is worth $250 or more, the recipient must disqualify themselves from participating in decisions with material financial effects on the donors. Specific Government Code sections (87207, 87300, 87313, 87100, 87103) and administrative code apply.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains clear Q&A structure. Letter format dated 1981 (file references 1982). No explicit section headers identified. Content structured as informal advice with legal citations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must a state director disclose and separately report out-of-state trips paid for by business groups or foreign governments, and what are the conflict of interest implications of accepting such trips?

CONCLUSION: Yes, such trips must be reported as gifts or income on the Statement of Economic Interests. If the trip is worth $250 or more, the recipient must disqualify themselves from participating in decisions with material financial effects on the donors. Specific Government Code sections (87207, 87300, 87313, 87100, 87103) and administrative code apply.

If a foreign government pays for such a trip that too would have to be disclosed as a gift or income. See Sections 87207, 87300, and 87313 of the Government Code, copies attached. In addition, if the trip is worth $250 or more, then the director of the international trade office would be required to disqualify himself or herself from participating in any decision which would have a material financial effect -- distinguishable from the public generally -- on the businesses or foreign government involved. embedding.summary: FPPC advises that out-of-state trips for a state director paid for by business groups or foreign governments must be disclosed as gifts or income, and trips valued at $250+ trigger mandatory disqualification from decisions benefiting the donors.
